title: Museums | USCIS
content:
The Families Learning Civics Together Toolkit is now available. The toolkit is a collection of games and resources for parents and children to explore civics and citizenship together.
Museums are an important part of their communities. Many museums provide support and opportunities for lifelong learning, making them an ideal source for citizenship education and outreach initiatives designed for immigrants interested in learning more about naturalization and to educate the general public about citizenship and immigration in the United States.
Museums often encourage civic engagement as well, which can include not only learning about U.S. history and government, but also about what it means to be an active and responsible citizen. Additionally, museums may have historical displays, collections, and artifacts that are relevant to civics education and to the themes and content that immigrants preparing for citizenship need to learn for the naturalization test.
Through our joint effort with the Institute of Museum and Library Services (IMLS ), we seek to support museums by offering public information and engagement opportunities, and by distributing educational materials and training resources on immigration and citizenship.

## What Museums Can Do to Participate
- Include exhibits on citizenship and immigration, such as a display on the immigration experience, as part of larger museum programming. Incorporate artifacts from your collection to tell the story.
- Consider establishing a Citizenship Corner .
- Explore the list of available citizenship education resources on the USCIS Citizenship Resource Center . Find study materials for the English language test and 2008 version of the civics test .
- Find study materials for the English language test and 2008 version of the civics test .
- Host USCIS administrative naturalization ceremonies . Many museums feature beautiful spaces and ideal locations for hosting naturalization ceremonies. Ceremonies can be hosted at a full range of museum types – not only historic houses and history museums that might have a direct connection to U.S. history and government themes – but also children’s museums and art museums, as well as aquariums, planetariums, and zoos. If you are interested in hosting a naturalization ceremony at your museum, contact your local USCIS community relations officer .
- If you are interested in hosting a naturalization ceremony at your museum, contact your local USCIS community relations officer .
- Participate in a teacher training. USCIS provides free training workshops for adult educators on how to teach U.S. history and civics to immigrants. Find out more information about upcoming training seminars . If you are interested in hosting a training event through your museum, contact the USCIS Office of Citizenship at office.of.citizenship@uscis.dhs.gov .
- Find out more information about upcoming training seminars .
- If you are interested in hosting a training event through your museum, contact the USCIS Office of Citizenship at office.of.citizenship@uscis.dhs.gov .
- Contact IMLS or your local USCIS community relations officer for further information and guidance on the support and opportunities available for museums.
- Sign up for free email alerts with important news and information from USCIS. First, enter your email address. Then, under the “Outreach” topic, put a check mark next to “Updates from USCIS-IMLS.”
